sentences.text: Well, the German refugees in the ghetto, they lived like pigs in the warehouses, except for the ones who had the money.
So they had-- they had houses on their own.
You see the conditions were not good.
Because they were not so rich.
But somehow they carried on.
And there was a street, Chusan Road, where there were all the shops.
There were grocers and so on.
And there was this Barcelona Cafe, where everybody sought some warming up against a combustion stove.
And there were discussions and strategy.
And we stuck to the radio.
We read the papers, you, see that means had a Russian paper.
Because in the meantime, you see, just being amongst Russians, we picked up Russian very fast.
And we had a Russian paper, edited by the Communist Party, and a Russian paper edited by the White Russian Society.
And we have just as well as two radio stations.
They-- we had a radio station run in Hongkew in the ghetto by the German refugees.
Pardon?
The German Jews.
And we have the English newspaper, you know, which was under control of Japanese, as it's censored.
And we had the German paper, issued by the Nazis.
And we knew everything.
You see there were outlets.
And when you passed the guards you had to have a pass.
And some people were lucky to get the passes, so it depends on the mood, you see.
For instance, I had to go to the doctor.
So I got a one-day pass.
And there were people who were paying to Japanese firms, to keep them on the payroll.
You see, and as Japanese employees, they could go back and forwards, or Chinese, you see sort of thing.
If you had enough money, and they were bringing in stuff on the way back.
And they were not selling directly.
Because there were people who picked up the merchandise from them, you see.
Oh, you had to queue up, you see, to Mr.
Ghoya or Mr.
Kubota.
And give them a reason.
And one day I went in, you see, he said you-- you see I was tall, you see that means the Japs were short.
And I was tall, so he say, you look as American, and he slapped me twice over cheeks, you see.
And he had to take it, you see.
And some people, sometimes, some people came over.
And the Japanese officer or something, he got up on the stool to beat up the fellow.
You see, and we just took it.
And not a single minute we thought as losers, but as victors, and probably this gave us the hope.
Pardon?
Oh, there were.
There were, you see.
That means that they were all diseased, you know, because first of all, there was dysentery.
You see, bacillus dysentery and amoebic dysentery.
And everybody had tape worms.
And you see, it was a sort of a routine that once a year you got yourself santonin, you see, that kills the worm.
And a sort of a laxative, to put them out.
And my last worms came out on the ship on the way to-- to Australia through the mouth.
You see, maybe two meters.
And since then, everything was all right.
And I had on top of it my TB.
And then there was [NON-ENGLISH], [NON-ENGLISH], sort of a-- lot of bugs.
Well, there was a Hongkew hospital that means the Jewish hospital.
And in the French Concession was a [NON-ENGLISH].
And they did whatever they could.
Indifferently.
You see, you-- you walk through the gate, he didn't even look at the pass and let you go.
And then the next day somebody walked through, and they beat him up.
You see just for fun.
So see, they're enemies.
And you see, well, a lot of Japanese-- of Chinese leaving amount.
Because it was not a ghetto that means only Jews.
And it was meant, you see, it was named a restricted area for all-- for alien refugees.
You see, now amongst us were a few Christians, a few Poles.
Well, they got an exemption through the church.
You understand?
So they were the same refugees, but there were exemptions.
There was two brothers who were agents for [NON-ENGLISH].
You see?
So they got themselves papers.
They traveled from Switzerland as businesspeople.
And they got stuck in Shanghai.
So they were all the time, they lived all the time in the French Concession.
One of the sons of the people is my doctor who operated on me, you know.
And you see everybody was trying, you see, that means-- that means I was picking up some merchandise from the people who had the passes and brought it.
And then we had the kitchen, you see, a Kosher kitchen who was supplying, was giving you see, a cauldron of soup.
Ladles.
You see, that means one ladle from the bottom, and one ladle from the top.
So nobody was cheated.
And as I told you before, you see, I had, you see the room on the attic.
So I picked up the soup in-- in the pot.
And I was carrying it to my-- we were putting in some lard or something, some fried onions to make it taste.
I think I had it all gone.
You see, because at the end of the ghetto, I got myself a job with American Army.
It happened in August, September '45.
In Shanghai?
I arrived at end of August or beginning of September 1941.
And I left for Australia in 1946.
Yeah.
I was in-- in the jail hospital?
Because, you see, now and again, they used to close street, and pick up a bunch of people, 10-20 for investigation, you see.
One day they were looking for spies.
And the other day they were looking for the black market, just-- just to end-- to-- to be active.
They didn't mean it seriously.
You see, because I was lucky.
So the day before the bombs, I was picked up from the street, and taken to this cell.
You know, and since I had on my card that I have TB, which was an old card from outside the ghetto, you see, a fellow, a criminal I knew, that Mr.
[NON-ENGLISH] had he started yelling to the guard that we have a man who has TB, and we'll all get infected or something.
So they put on these cuffs on me, and put-- and send me to the hospital.
Was this outside of the restricted area?
No.
This was within, you see.
That this-- that was still there, I just was in jail or something.
And you see that means I was busy, you see, killing bed bugs.
All the time, there was-- you see they were jumping from heaven all the mattresses.
And you know, the-- then you see I had the bumps.
And I say, I saw that there were bringing the wounded to the hospital, and they were operating them on, on the courtyard, at the end of the hospital.
And you see this running with bottles of some anesthetics and things, and they're cutting off legs, and I was looking at that.
And the rest of the time I was killing bugs.
And then there was from the old English time, a policeman, a Hindu, a Sikh with a turban or something like this.
So I sent him a message, send with him a message to my wife.
And he took the message.
And he told them the story that he lives out of town, and he has a cow.
And then we finished up a supplier of milk.
You see, we used to keep the milk, you see, to get sour.
And the top off this was cream.
You see, so we're in a bottle, shook it to make butter and from the rest we made cottage cheese.
And you see, piece by piece, together.
And another time I bought a box of salmon, and kept it, and sold it and made them few bob.
You see that means I bit speculated.
And it's what they say.
The Japs were fighting the Chinese.
They're fighting the communists on one front, and Shanghai Czech on the other front.
And you see roads were cut and opened.
So the roads opened up.
So out of [NON-ENGLISH] came transports of army.
So a fellow who had, you see, they pretend also something like this, got himself sort of a [NON-ENGLISH] of honey, and was selling to France little jars of honey.
So he had enough honey for himself.
And you see, just sort of an existence.
And--
You know.
Yeah.
You see, we were sitting as I told you, in the Barcelona Cafe.
And some people had short-wave radios.
And we knew everything about the Solomon battle and the Midway battle.
No, illegally.
They came with the stories.
And then there was Okinawa.
And after the Americans took over Okinawa, they started-- they picked up people, you see, from the streets with forced labor, and they started digging machine guns nets-- nests.
You know?
In Shanghai, in case the next offensive is Shanghai, because it was the nearest to Okinawa.
And we knew everything.
You see, that means this Barcelona Cafe was a-- a source of information and everything.
And then in-- on the 6th of August, you see the important dates I remember-- on the 6th of August, there was the atomic bomb on Hiroshima.
And I didn't know what the difference between the one bomb and the other.
But amongst our group there were chemists and-- and physicists or something, and were discussing how was the atomic bomb, et cetera, et cetera.
And before you knew, two days later, there was another bomb on Nagasaki.
And there was gossip that the Japanese surrendered.
So some boys from the [NON-ENGLISH], you see, the military fellow scouts, you see they went and occupied the-- the police station, you know.
And they found out that there is no surrender.
So they ran quickly away.
And then you see we had at 2 o'clock or whatever it is in the afternoon, there will be through the English-speaking station, they will be an announcement of the Emperor.
And you see, it was broadcasted, and at the same time was translated into the English.
And the Emperor, as you see, had his speech.
And we say, oh.
So we quickly ran to Barcelona, we got ourselves drunk.
And funny thing, we were so happy, everybody, that we didn't think of the people back in Europe.
You see, and-- because at that time the Germans surrendered in May.
And at that time, we already knew unfortunately about Treblinka and about Buchenwald, about Auschwitz.
And you see, we were sort of in mourning, you see.
Because everybody, you see, there were some people man and woman live together, she escaped with her husband left in Warsaw, and he escaped with a wife left in Lodz or something.
They lived together with an agreement if the husband's-- if the partner, the spouses survive, so we finish.
And a few, you see, had to break up, because the spouses survived.
And a few got married.
And anyway, the speech ended that there is the American army ordered the Japs keep control over the town until the American Marines come over.
You see, so the next day a few officers came over by plane.
And you see, Japs still had the sentries and the guards.
So, and then they waited until the big ship comes with the marines.
Some people were very smart.
They started to go to the Japanese district and buy a lot of merchandise.
You know, because they were in a big panic, and made a lot of money.
A friend of mine, you see, he-- he was dealing with the Japs.
He was buying all the mercury quicksilver.
You know, I said, look Henry, what-- what are you doing?
When-- when the war is over, they come and shoot you.
I said, I don't care.
Well this fellow with his money, he got himself a suite in the Palace Hotel, you see, with a big stock of whiskey and brandy .
And immediately when the high brass came over, he started business with the Americans.
Wouldn't tell you who it is, you see.
But, you see, he was very frank.
What he said was money is money.
And the people, the worst people, you know that means they-- they became privileged American, because they were ready for it.
Well, I got myself a job with the post exchange, with the American.
You see, that means they had to go.
They gave me an exam of an accountant.
And said that I was speaking English, and no, not very correctly, but still communicating.
And I was the first.
Because I was very qualified.
I was the first for the papers, so they gave me the most important job.
So they gave me the job with exchange service, you see, with China, the replacement service.
You see, the headquarters where people-- where GIs were sent home, and new GIs were coming.
You see, there was a sort of a billet, you know, for the transit people.
And everybody spoke English, you see.
And my commanding officer was from South Carolina.
I didn't understand him.
I didn't understand my English, you see, because my English was mixed, a bit of this and a bit of that.
And mostly, you see, I was expressing while I was spelling Latin or something.
And we had to write to each other.
But eventually, you see, that means it took a few days, you see, while you are in the crowd, I started registering all the thing.
I was a very good friend with the major, Major Johnson.
And I opened up a gift shop, and put Lola into it.
You know, so she made an extra few bob, and we made enough money, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] you know, which later made us a little start in Australia.
At that time, I know there's nothing to come to.
Because at that time, you see that means between the surrender in Europe, we found out that there was a revolution in Warsaw, you see, an uprising.
And the ghetto is gone.
And then there was an uprising when the Russians were sitting on the East Bank, and watching like the rest of the town was.
[INAUDIBLE].
And I was trying to get, you see, somewhere, you see, that means I had one brother in Sao Paulo.
And I had one brother, you see, the brother who went to Burma.
He ended up in Lahore, in Pakistan.
And I thought they would help me.
And my clever brother in Pakistan had friends who migrated to Australia in 1938.
And this friend fixed me up with a permit.
I arrived in Australia in September.
Now we left in June on a-- on a troop transport ship, General Gordon, in [NON-ENGLISH], and went to Hong Kong.
In Hong Kong the Peninsula Hotel that Mr.
Kadoorie, who was-- his father was Jewish, he offered the two ballrooms.
He offered two ballrooms to the refugees.
So there was a ballroom one for man on stretchers, and one for women.
And a few days later, we were suppose to-- to board a ship, you see, Her Majesty's Duntroon, to take us to Australia.
There was a group of 300 people.
And when we came to Hong Kong, we find out that the trip of the Duntroon was canceled.
What we found out later-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] that the ship was canceled on account of the elections in Australia.
And Mr.
Chifley was afraid that in one lot, if he takes in 300 jews, and the press will pick up the news, he might lose the election.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
And we got stuck in the two ballrooms.
How long were you there for?
A couple months.
Playing around, you see, that means we make a connection with the American-- the English Army.
There was a fellow foreman, who was running a troop, an English entertaining troop, and he was playing the-- The Bumblebee, and et cetera.
And we went together, and then you see that means he was going with an entertainer for the English Army, but he was doing big black market.
And-- and-- that means we-- there was a strike on the ferry, between Kowloon and the island.
And we used to hire boats and go in, in there, and singing and-- and happy.
Pardon?
Oh, well an experience, you see, that means like you see we were still young.
You see, I was 31, but I-- I missed the 5-6 years.
So I was sort of still young, like we were in a camp, you know.
Now mister-- we found, you see, that means we, being looked after by the Russian Jews, we learned Russian in the peninsula had a manager, a Russian manager.
And he gave us a room, and we were making parties, the Polish Group.
You see, there was a captain, a Jewish nationality, a Jewish captain of the American army who was running our group, the 300 people from Burma.
And he lined up two freighters who were going back to Australia without freight.
And they just build sort of cots, you see, in the-- in the hold.
[NON-ENGLISH].
I don't know.
Something like more-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] it just escaped my mind.
